name: Materiable
short_description: A pin-based shape-changing display that renders dynamic material properties such as flexibility, elasticity, and viscosity in response to a user's direct touch.
long_description: Materiable is a research prototype from MIT Media Lab's Tangible Media Group, presented at CHI 2016, that uses a pin-based shape display to make digital data feel like physical material. Rather than rendering only static shapes, it runs physics algorithms (including shallow-water equations for liquids and spring models for deformable solids) so that pressing on the surface elicits material-like responses, letting people perceive flexibility, elasticity, and viscosity through touch. As physical AI / interaction design, it matters because it extends shape-changing interfaces from shape alone toward rich, tangible material sensation, advancing how computational surfaces can communicate haptic and dynamic qualities to the body.
ai_note: The system is driven by hand-authored physics simulations (shallow-water equations and spring models) on a motorized pin display, with no AI or machine learning involved.

images.caption: A user presses into the Materiable pin-based shape display, which responds with liquid- and material-like deformation rendered by physics algorithms.
images.alt_text: A hand pressing down on a grid of motorized white pins that form a deformable surface, with the pins around the touch point lowered to mimic a soft, yielding material.

sources.title: Materiable
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab Tangible Media Group

sources.title: Materiable: Rendering Dynamic Material Properties in Response to Direct Physical Touch with Shape Changing Interfaces
sources.publisher: Proceedings of the 2016 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (ACM)
